questions.title: Definition of Divisibility
questions.description: Which of the following is the correct definition of 'a divides b' (written a | b)?
options: There exists an integer c such that b = ac
There exists an integer c such that a = bc
The remainder when b is divided by a is 1
a is less than or equal to b
questions.explanation: By definition, a | b (a divides b) means there exists an integer c such that b = ac. For example, 3 | 12 because 12 = 3 * 4.
hints: Think about what it means for 3 to divide 12
The divisor multiplied by some integer should give the dividend

questions.title: Divisibility Check
questions.description: Which of the following statements is TRUE?
questions.explanation: 7 | 42 is true because 42 = 7 * 6. For the others: 37 = 5 * 7 + 2 (remainder 2), 44 = 3 * 14 + 2 (remainder 2), 50 = 8 * 6 + 2 (remainder 2).
hints: Check which division gives a remainder of zero
a | b means b/a has no remainder

questions.title: Prime Number Identification
questions.description: Which of the following numbers is prime?
questions.explanation: 67 is prime (divisible only by 1 and 67). 51 = 3 * 17, 91 = 7 * 13, and 87 = 3 * 29, so none of these are prime.
hints: A prime number has exactly two divisors: 1 and itself
Try dividing each number by small primes (2, 3, 5, 7, ...)

questions.title: Primtal (Danish)
questions.description: Hvor mange primtal er der mellem 1 og 20 (begge eksklusiv)?
questions.explanation: Primtallene mellem 1 og 20 er: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19. Det giver 8 primtal. Husk at 1 ikke er et primtal, og 2 er det eneste lige primtal.
hints: Husk at 1 ikke er et primtal
2 er det mindste primtal

questions.title: GCD Basic Computation
questions.description: What is gcd(24, 36)?
questions.explanation: The divisors of 24 are {1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24} and the divisors of 36 are {1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, 36}. The greatest common divisor is 12. Alternatively, 24 = 2^3 * 3 and 36 = 2^2 * 3^2, so gcd = 2^2 * 3 = 12.
hints: Find the prime factorization of both numbers
The GCD uses the minimum power of each common prime factor

questions.title: Euclid's Algorithm Step-by-Step
questions.description: When computing gcd(252, 198) using Euclid's algorithm, what is the sequence of remainders?
questions.explanation: Euclid's algorithm: 252 = 1 * 198 + 54, then 198 = 3 * 54 + 36, then 54 = 1 * 36 + 18, then 36 = 2 * 18 + 0. The sequence of remainders is 54, 36, 18, 0, and gcd(252, 198) = 18.
hints: At each step, divide the larger number by the smaller and take the remainder
Continue until the remainder is 0; the last nonzero remainder is the GCD

questions.title: GCD via Euclid's Algorithm
questions.description: What is gcd(462, 1071) computed using Euclid's algorithm?
hints: Start by dividing 1071 by 462
Keep applying the division algorithm until the remainder is 0

questions.title: LCM Basic Computation
questions.description: What is lcm(12, 18)?
hints: Use the formula: lcm(a,b) = a*b / gcd(a,b)
First find gcd(12, 18)

questions.title: GCD and LCM Relationship
questions.description: If gcd(a, b) = 14 and lcm(a, b) = 168, what is the product a * b?
questions.explanation: The fundamental identity states that gcd(a,b) * lcm(a,b) = a * b. Therefore a * b = 14 * 168 = 2352.
hints: There is a well-known relationship connecting gcd, lcm, and the product of two numbers
gcd(a,b) * lcm(a,b) = a * b

questions.title: Congruence Basics
questions.description: Which of the following congruences is TRUE?
questions.explanation: 17 mod 3 = 2, since 17 = 5 * 3 + 2. Therefore 17 ≡ 2 (mod 3) is the correct congruence. Checking the others: 17 mod 3 = 2 (not 5), 17 mod 5 = 2 (not 3), 17 mod 5 = 2 (not 1).
hints: a ≡ b (mod m) means m divides (a - b)
Find the remainder when 17 is divided by the modulus

questions.title: Kongruens (Danish)
questions.description: Hvad er 47 mod 7?
questions.explanation: 47 = 7 * 6 + 5, så 47 mod 7 = 5. Resten ved division af 47 med 7 er 5.
hints: Del 47 med 7 og find resten

questions.title: Modular Arithmetic: Addition
questions.description: In Z_7 (integers modulo 7), what is (5 + 6) mod 7?
questions.explanation: 5 + 6 = 11, and 11 mod 7 = 4 (since 11 = 1 * 7 + 4). In modular arithmetic, we always reduce the result modulo m.
hints: First compute 5 + 6, then reduce modulo 7

questions.title: Modular Arithmetic: Multiplication
questions.description: What is (13 * 17) mod 10?
questions.explanation: 13 * 17 = 221, and 221 mod 10 = 1. Alternatively, we can compute (13 mod 10) * (17 mod 10) = 3 * 7 = 21, and 21 mod 10 = 1.
hints: You can reduce each factor modulo 10 before multiplying
(a * b) mod m = ((a mod m) * (b mod m)) mod m

questions.title: Modular Inverse
questions.description: What is the multiplicative inverse of 3 modulo 11?
questions.explanation: We need to find x such that 3x ≡ 1 (mod 11). Testing: 3 * 4 = 12 = 11 + 1, so 3 * 4 ≡ 1 (mod 11). Therefore, the inverse of 3 modulo 11 is 4.
hints: Find x such that 3x ≡ 1 (mod 11)
Try multiplying 3 by each option and check if the result is congruent to 1 mod 11

questions.title: Existence of Modular Inverse
questions.description: For which value of a does a multiplicative inverse of a modulo 15 NOT exist?
questions.explanation: A multiplicative inverse of a modulo m exists if and only if gcd(a, m) = 1. gcd(2, 15) = 1, gcd(4, 15) = 1, gcd(6, 15) = 3 ≠ 1, gcd(7, 15) = 1. So a = 6 has no inverse modulo 15.
hints: The inverse of a mod m exists iff gcd(a, m) = 1
Check which value shares a common factor with 15

questions.title: Solving a Linear Congruence
questions.description: Find the solution to 5x ≡ 3 (mod 7).
questions.explanation: First find the inverse of 5 modulo 7. Since 5 * 3 = 15 ≡ 1 (mod 7), the inverse of 5 is 3. Then x ≡ 3 * 3 = 9 ≡ 2 (mod 7).
hints: First find the multiplicative inverse of 5 modulo 7
Then multiply both sides of the congruence by the inverse

questions.title: Chinese Remainder Theorem: Simple System
questions.description: Solve the system: x ≡ 2 (mod 3) and x ≡ 3 (mod 5). What is the unique solution modulo 15?
questions.explanation: We need x such that x ≡ 2 (mod 3) and x ≡ 3 (mod 5). Testing x = 8: 8 mod 3 = 2 (correct) and 8 mod 5 = 3 (correct). By CRT, the solution is unique modulo 15 (since gcd(3,5) = 1), so x ≡ 8 (mod 15).
hints: Since gcd(3,5) = 1, the CRT guarantees a unique solution modulo 15
Try listing numbers that satisfy x ≡ 3 (mod 5): 3, 8, 13, ... and check which also satisfies x ≡ 2 (mod 3)

questions.title: Chinese Remainder Theorem: Three Congruences
questions.description: Solve the system: x ≡ 1 (mod 2), x ≡ 2 (mod 3), x ≡ 3 (mod 5). What is x mod 30?
questions.explanation: We can verify x = 23: 23 mod 2 = 1 (correct), 23 mod 3 = 2 (correct), 23 mod 5 = 3 (correct). By the CRT (since 2, 3, 5 are pairwise coprime), the unique solution modulo 30 is x ≡ 23 (mod 30).
hints: The moduli 2, 3, 5 are pairwise coprime, so CRT applies with modulus 2*3*5 = 30
Try listing numbers that satisfy x ≡ 3 (mod 5) and are odd: 3, 13, 23, ... then check mod 3

questions.title: Fermat's Little Theorem: Statement
questions.description: According to Fermat's little theorem, if p = 13 and gcd(a, 13) = 1, what is a^12 mod 13?
questions.explanation: Fermat's little theorem states that if p is prime and gcd(a, p) = 1, then a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p). Here p = 13, so a^12 ≡ 1 (mod 13).
hints: Fermat's little theorem involves a^(p-1) mod p
Since p = 13, we have p - 1 = 12

questions.title: Fermat's Little Theorem: Computation
questions.description: Using Fermat's little theorem, what is 2^340 mod 11?
questions.explanation: By Fermat's little theorem, since 11 is prime and gcd(2, 11) = 1, we have 2^10 ≡ 1 (mod 11). Since 340 = 34 * 10, we get 2^340 = (2^10)^34 ≡ 1^34 = 1 (mod 11).
hints: By Fermat's little theorem, 2^10 ≡ 1 (mod 11)
Express 340 as a multiple of 10

questions.title: Divisibility Properties
questions.description: Which of the following statements about divisibility are TRUE? (Select all that apply)
options: If a | b and a | c, then a | (b + c)
If a | b and b | c, then a | c (transitivity)
If a | b, then b | a
If a | bc, then a | b or a | c
questions.explanation: A is true: if b = ak and c = al, then b + c = a(k + l), so a | (b + c). B is true (transitivity of divisibility): if b = ak and c = bl, then c = akl, so a | c. C is false: e.g. 3 | 6 but 6 does not divide 3. D is false in general: e.g. 6 | (4 * 3) but 6 does not divide 4 and 6 does not divide 3. (D is true only if a is prime.)
hints: Use the definition: a | b means b = a * k for some integer k
Think of specific counterexamples for the false statements

questions.title: Properties of GCD
questions.description: Which of the following are always true for positive integers a and b? (Select all that apply)
options: gcd(a, b) divides both a and b
gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b) when b > 0
questions.explanation: All four statements are true. A: By definition, the GCD is a common divisor. B: Since gcd(a,b) divides both a and b, it must be at most the smaller of the two. C: Every integer divides 0, so the greatest common divisor of a and 0 is a. D: This is the fundamental property that makes Euclid's algorithm work.
hints: Think about what 'greatest common divisor' means by definition
Statement D is the key recursion in Euclid's algorithm

questions.title: Egenskaber ved kongruenser (Danish)
questions.description: Hvilke af nedenstående udsagn om kongruenser er SANDE? (Vælg alle korrekte)
options: Hvis a ≡ b (mod m), så er m | (a - b)
Hvis a ≡ b (mod m) og c ≡ d (mod m), så er a + c ≡ b + d (mod m)
Hvis a ≡ b (mod m) og c ≡ d (mod m), så er ac ≡ bd (mod m)
Hvis ac ≡ bc (mod m), så er a ≡ b (mod m)
questions.explanation: A, B, og C er alle sande egenskaber ved kongruenser. D er FALSK i generel form - man kan kun dividere med c hvis gcd(c, m) = 1. Modeksempel: 2*3 ≡ 2*0 (mod 6) men 3 er ikke kongruent med 0 (mod 6).
hints: Kongruenser bevarer addition og multiplikation
Vær forsigtig med division/cancellation i modulær aritmetik

questions.title: When Does a Modular Inverse Exist?
questions.description: For which of the following values of a does a^(-1) (mod 12) exist? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: The inverse of a modulo 12 exists iff gcd(a, 12) = 1. gcd(5, 12) = 1, gcd(7, 12) = 1, gcd(8, 12) = 4, gcd(9, 12) = 3. So only a = 5 and a = 7 have inverses modulo 12.
hints: A modular inverse exists if and only if gcd(a, m) = 1
12 = 2^2 * 3, so any a sharing a factor of 2 or 3 with 12 has no inverse

questions.title: Conditions for CRT Applicability
questions.description: The Chinese Remainder Theorem guarantees a unique solution modulo M = m_1 * m_2 * ... * m_k when which conditions hold? (Select all that apply)
options: The moduli m_1, m_2, ..., m_k are pairwise coprime
The moduli m_1, m_2, ..., m_k are all prime numbers
Each individual congruence x ≡ a_i (mod m_i) has a solution
The remainders a_1, a_2, ..., a_k are pairwise distinct
questions.explanation: The CRT requires that the moduli are pairwise coprime (A). Each congruence x ≡ a_i (mod m_i) trivially has solutions (any a_i works), so C is always satisfied and is part of the setup. B is sufficient but not necessary (e.g., moduli 4 and 9 are coprime but not prime). D is not required (the remainders can repeat).
hints: The key condition for CRT is about the moduli, not the remainders
Pairwise coprime means gcd(m_i, m_j) = 1 for all i ≠ j

questions.title: Division Algorithm
questions.description: The Division Algorithm states: for integers a and d with d > 0, there exist unique integers q and r such that a = d * q + r and {blank1} ≤ r < {blank2}.
questions.explanation: The Division Algorithm guarantees unique integers q (quotient) and r (remainder) such that a = dq + r with 0 ≤ r < d. The remainder is always nonnegative and strictly less than the divisor.
hints: The remainder must be nonnegative
The remainder must be strictly less than the divisor

questions.title: Fermat's Little Theorem
questions.description: By Fermat's little theorem, if p is prime and gcd(a, p) = 1, then a^{blank1} ≡ {blank2} (mod p).
questions.explanation: Fermat's little theorem states that if p is prime and gcd(a, p) = 1, then a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p). This is a fundamental result in number theory with many applications including primality testing and cryptography.
hints: The exponent involves p minus something
The result of a^(p-1) modulo p is always the multiplicative identity

questions.title: GCD-LCM Relationship
questions.description: For positive integers a and b: gcd(a, b) * {blank1} = {blank2}.
questions.explanation: The fundamental relationship between GCD and LCM is: gcd(a, b) * lcm(a, b) = a * b. This formula is extremely useful for computing the LCM when the GCD is known.
hints: Think about the prime factorizations and how min/max of exponents relate to sum of exponents
This identity lets you compute LCM from GCD easily

questions.title: Euclid's Algorithm Key Step
questions.description: Euclid's algorithm is based on the identity: gcd(a, b) = gcd({blank1}, {blank2}) when b > 0.
questions.explanation: The key identity behind Euclid's algorithm is gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b). This reduces the problem to smaller numbers at each step, eventually reaching gcd(d, 0) = d.
hints: The arguments swap and the remainder replaces the original first argument
The modulo operation is the key to reducing the problem size

questions.title: Congruence Definition
questions.description: a ≡ b (mod m) means that m divides {blank1}, or equivalently, a and b have the same {blank2} when divided by m.
questions.explanation: a ≡ b (mod m) means m | (a - b), which is equivalent to saying that a and b leave the same remainder when divided by m.
hints: Think about what it means for two numbers to be 'equivalent' modulo m
17 ≡ 5 (mod 4) because both leave remainder 1 when divided by 4

questions.title: Extended Euclidean Algorithm
questions.description: Using the extended Euclidean algorithm on gcd(35, 15), which of the following correctly expresses gcd(35, 15) as a linear combination 35s + 15t?
questions.explanation: First, gcd(35, 15): 35 = 2 * 15 + 5, then 15 = 3 * 5 + 0, so gcd = 5. Back-substituting: 5 = 35 - 2 * 15 = 35(1) + 15(-2). We verify: 35 - 30 = 5. Correct!
hints: First run Euclid's algorithm forward, then back-substitute to express the GCD as a linear combination
Bezout's identity guarantees integers s, t with gcd(a,b) = as + bt

questions.title: Linear Congruence: Multiple Solutions
questions.description: How many solutions does the congruence 6x ≡ 4 (mod 10) have modulo 10?
questions.explanation: The congruence ax ≡ b (mod m) has solutions iff gcd(a, m) | b. Here gcd(6, 10) = 2 and 2 | 4, so solutions exist. The number of solutions modulo 10 is gcd(6, 10) = 2. Dividing through by 2: 3x ≡ 2 (mod 5). The inverse of 3 mod 5 is 2 (since 3*2=6≡1), so x ≡ 4 (mod 5). The two solutions mod 10 are x = 4 and x = 9.
hints: The number of solutions of ax ≡ b (mod m) is gcd(a, m) if gcd(a, m) | b, otherwise 0
First check if gcd(6, 10) divides 4

questions.title: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
questions.description: What is the prime factorization of 360?
questions.explanation: 360 = 2 * 180 = 2 * 2 * 90 = 2 * 2 * 2 * 45 = 2^3 * 45 = 2^3 * 9 * 5 = 2^3 * 3^2 * 5. Option B uses 10 which is not prime. Option C is not in standard form. Option D uses 45 which is not prime.
hints: Keep dividing by the smallest prime possible
The factorization must use only prime numbers

questions.title: Fermat's Little Theorem: Finding Inverse
questions.description: Using Fermat's little theorem, what is 5^(-1) mod 7 (the multiplicative inverse of 5 modulo 7)?
questions.explanation: By Fermat's little theorem, 5^6 ≡ 1 (mod 7), so 5^5 ≡ 5^(-1) (mod 7). We compute: 5^2 = 25 ≡ 4 (mod 7), 5^4 ≡ 4^2 = 16 ≡ 2 (mod 7), 5^5 ≡ 2 * 5 = 10 ≡ 3 (mod 7). Verification: 5 * 3 = 15 ≡ 1 (mod 7). Correct!
hints: By Fermat's little theorem, a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p), so a^(-1) ≡ a^(p-2) (mod p)
Compute 5^5 mod 7 using repeated squaring

questions.title: Coprime Numbers
questions.description: Which pair of numbers is coprime (relatively prime)?
questions.explanation: Two numbers are coprime if their GCD is 1. gcd(14, 21) = 7, gcd(15, 28) = 1 (since 15 = 3 * 5 and 28 = 2^2 * 7 share no common prime factors), gcd(12, 18) = 6, gcd(9, 24) = 3. Only 15 and 28 are coprime.
hints: Find the GCD of each pair
Coprime means gcd = 1 (no common prime factors)

questions.title: Modular Exponentiation
questions.description: What is 3^100 mod 7?
questions.explanation: By Fermat's little theorem, 3^6 ≡ 1 (mod 7). Now 100 = 16 * 6 + 4, so 3^100 = (3^6)^16 * 3^4 ≡ 1^16 * 3^4 (mod 7). We compute 3^4 = 81 = 11 * 7 + 4, so 3^4 ≡ 4 (mod 7). Therefore 3^100 ≡ 4 (mod 7).
hints: Use Fermat's little theorem: 3^6 ≡ 1 (mod 7)
Write 100 = 6q + r and use the theorem to simplify

questions.title: CRT Prerequisites
questions.description: Consider the system: x ≡ 3 (mod 6) and x ≡ 5 (mod 10). Which of the following are true? (Select all that apply)
options: The standard CRT cannot be directly applied since gcd(6, 10) ≠ 1
The system can still have solutions if the congruences are compatible
questions.explanation: gcd(6, 10) = 2, so the moduli are not coprime and the standard CRT does not directly apply (A is false, B is true). However, a system with non-coprime moduli can still have solutions if the congruences are compatible: we need 3 ≡ 5 (mod gcd(6,10)), i.e., 3 ≡ 5 (mod 2). Since 3 mod 2 = 1 and 5 mod 2 = 1, they are compatible, so solutions exist (C is false, D is true). For example, x = 15 works: 15 mod 6 = 3 and 15 mod 10 = 5.
hints: First check if gcd(6, 10) = 1
When moduli are not coprime, solutions can still exist if the remainders are consistent modulo the GCD

questions.title: GCD Computation with Large Numbers
questions.description: What is gcd(144, 89)?
questions.explanation: Using Euclid's algorithm: 144 = 1 * 89 + 55, then 89 = 1 * 55 + 34, then 55 = 1 * 34 + 21, then 34 = 1 * 21 + 13, then 21 = 1 * 13 + 8, then 13 = 1 * 8 + 5, then 8 = 1 * 5 + 3, then 5 = 1 * 3 + 2, then 3 = 1 * 2 + 1, then 2 = 2 * 1 + 0. So gcd(144, 89) = 1. (Note: 89 and 144 are consecutive Fibonacci numbers, which are always coprime.)
hints: These are consecutive Fibonacci numbers
Apply Euclid's algorithm - consecutive Fibonacci numbers have a special property

questions.title: Bezout's Identity
questions.description: Bezout's identity states that for any integers a and b (not both zero), there exist integers s and t such that {blank1} = gcd(a, b). The values s and t can be found using the {blank2} algorithm.
questions.explanation: Bezout's identity states gcd(a, b) = as + bt for some integers s and t. These coefficients can be found by running Euclid's algorithm forward and then back-substituting, a process known as the extended Euclidean algorithm.
hints: The GCD can always be expressed as a linear combination of a and b
The algorithm is an extension of the standard Euclidean algorithm

questions.title: Chinese Remainder Theorem Statement
questions.description: The Chinese Remainder Theorem states that if m_1, m_2, ..., m_k are pairwise {blank1}, then the system x ≡ a_i (mod m_i) for i = 1, ..., k has a {blank2} solution modulo M = m_1 * m_2 * ... * m_k.
questions.explanation: The CRT requires the moduli to be pairwise coprime (gcd(m_i, m_j) = 1 for all i ≠ j). Under this condition, the system has a unique solution modulo M = m_1 * m_2 * ... * m_k.
hints: The key condition is about the relationship between every pair of moduli
The uniqueness is modulo the product of all moduli

questions.title: Solving a System of Congruences (CRT)
questions.description: Solve the system: x ≡ 2 (mod 5), x ≡ 3 (mod 7). What is x mod 35?
hints: Apply the CRT construction: x = sum of a_i * M_i * y_i
You need to find the inverses of M_i modulo m_i

questions.title: Sieve of Eratosthenes
questions.description: When finding all primes up to 100 using the Sieve of Eratosthenes, what is the largest prime whose multiples you need to cross out?
questions.explanation: You only need to cross out multiples of primes up to sqrt(100) = 10. The primes up to 10 are 2, 3, 5, 7. So 7 is the largest prime whose multiples need to be crossed out. After crossing out multiples of 2, 3, 5, and 7, all remaining numbers up to 100 are prime.
hints: You only need to sieve up to sqrt(n)
What is sqrt(100)?

questions.title: Basic Modular Arithmetic
questions.description: What is (-7) mod 5?
questions.explanation: By the division algorithm, -7 = (-2) * 5 + 3, so the remainder is 3. In number theory, the mod operation always returns a nonnegative result: -7 mod 5 = 3 (since -7 ≡ 3 (mod 5) and 0 ≤ 3 < 5).
hints: The remainder must satisfy 0 ≤ r < 5
-7 + 10 = 3, and 10 is a multiple of 5

questions.title: Properties of Prime Numbers
questions.description: Which of the following statements about prime numbers are TRUE? (Select all that apply)
options: 2 is the only even prime number
There are infinitely many prime numbers
Every integer greater than 1 is either prime or can be written as a product of primes
The sum of two primes is always prime
questions.explanation: A: 2 is the only even prime (all other even numbers are divisible by 2). B: Euclid proved there are infinitely many primes. C: This is the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. D: Counterexample: 3 + 5 = 8, which is not prime.
hints: Think about why no even number other than 2 can be prime
For D, try adding small primes together

questions.title: Computing with Fermat's Little Theorem
questions.description: What is 7^225 mod 11?
hints: By Fermat's little theorem, 7^10 ≡ 1 (mod 11)
Write 225 = q * 10 + r and simplify

questions.title: Modular Inverse via Extended Euclidean
questions.description: What is 7^(-1) mod 15? (The multiplicative inverse of 7 modulo 15)
questions.explanation: We need x such that 7x ≡ 1 (mod 15). Testing: 7 * 13 = 91 = 6 * 15 + 1, so 7 * 13 ≡ 1 (mod 15). Therefore 7^(-1) ≡ 13 (mod 15). Alternatively, using the extended Euclidean algorithm: 15 = 2*7 + 1, so 1 = 15 - 2*7, giving 7*(-2) ≡ 1 (mod 15), and -2 ≡ 13 (mod 15).
hints: Find x such that 7x leaves remainder 1 when divided by 15
You can use the extended Euclidean algorithm or try each option

questions.title: Linear Congruence Solvability
questions.description: The linear congruence ax ≡ b (mod m) has a solution if and only if {blank1} divides b. When a solution exists, there are exactly {blank2} incongruent solutions modulo m.
questions.explanation: The congruence ax ≡ b (mod m) has solutions iff gcd(a, m) | b. When solutions exist, there are exactly gcd(a, m) incongruent solutions modulo m. If gcd(a, m) = 1, there is a unique solution.
hints: The same expression appears in both the existence condition and the count
When gcd(a, m) = 1, the solution is unique

questions.title: GCD Using Prime Factorization
questions.explanation: gcd is found by taking the minimum power of each common prime: min(3,2)=2 for prime 2, min(1,3)=1 for prime 3, min(2,1)=1 for prime 5. So gcd = 2^2 * 3^1 * 5^1 = 4 * 3 * 5 = 60.
hints: For each prime factor, take the minimum of the two exponents
gcd(p^a * q^b, p^c * q^d) = p^min(a,c) * q^min(b,d)

questions.title: LCM Using Prime Factorization
questions.explanation: lcm is found by taking the maximum power of each prime: max(3,2)=3 for prime 2, max(1,3)=3 for prime 3, max(2,1)=2 for prime 5. So lcm = 2^3 * 3^3 * 5^2 = 8 * 27 * 25 = 5400. Alternatively: lcm = 600 * 540 / gcd(600, 540) = 324000 / 60 = 5400.
hints: For each prime factor, take the maximum of the two exponents
You can verify using: lcm(a,b) = a*b / gcd(a,b)

questions.title: Fermat's Little Theorem: Conditions
questions.description: Which of the following are necessary conditions for Fermat's little theorem (a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p)) to hold? (Select all that apply)
options: p must be a prime number
a must be a positive integer
gcd(a, p) = 1 (i.e., p does not divide a)
a must be less than p
questions.explanation: Fermat's little theorem requires: (A) p is prime, and (C) gcd(a, p) = 1 (equivalently, p does not divide a). The theorem holds for any integer a (not just positive), so B is not necessary. And a does not need to be less than p (e.g., 8^6 ≡ 1 (mod 7) with a=8 > p=7), so D is not required.
hints: The theorem has two essential conditions on p and on the relationship between a and p
a can be any integer, as long as it's not divisible by p
